ITERATIVE POLICY LEARNING IN END-TO-END TRAINABLE TASK-ORIENTED NEURAL DIALOG MODELS

Bing Liu 1 , Ian Lane 1 , 2
1 Electrical and Computer Engineering, Carnegie Mellon University 2 Language Technologies Institute, Carnegie Mellon University
liubing@cmu.edu, lane@cmu.

bfb60ee8-df16-4be7-a09c-f4af24c8cf5b.label: text_chunk
bfb60ee8-df16-4be7-a09c-f4af24c8cf5b.text:
edu

ABSTRACT

In this paper, we present a deep reinforcement learning (RL) framework for iterative dialog policy optimization in end- to-end task-oriented dialog systems. Popular approaches in learning dialog policy with RL include letting a dialog agent to learn against a user simulator. Building a reliable user simulator, however, is not trivial, often as difficult as building a good dialog agent.

18238d56-5706-4c51-b8d3-d41d1ab2a9a2.label: text_chunk
18238d56-5706-4c51-b8d3-d41d1ab2a9a2.text:
We address this challenge by jointly optimizing the dialog agent and the user simulator with deep RL by simulating dialogs between the two agents.

 We first bootstrap a basic dialog agent and a basic user simulator by learning directly from dialog corpora with supervised train- ing. We then improve them further by letting the two agents to conduct task-oriented dialogs and iteratively optimizing their policies with deep RL.

da18b6f1-84ad-45af-959f-8453376aa2d1.label: text_chunk
da18b6f1-84ad-45af-959f-8453376aa2d1.text:
Both the dialog agent and the user simulator are designed with neural network models that can be trained end-to-end.

 Our experiment results show that the proposed method leads to promising improvements on task success rate and total task reward comparing to supervised training and single-agent RL training baseline models.Index Terms — dialog systems, task-oriented, dialog pol- icy, end-to-end, reinforcement learning

1.

39cc0064-7c3e-449d-90a5-bf969a31bdba.label: text_chunk
39cc0064-7c3e-449d-90a5-bf969a31bdba.text:
INTRODUCTION

Task-oriented dialog system is playing an increasingly impor- tant role in enabling human-computer interactions via natu- ral spoken language. Different from chatbot type of conver- sational agents [1, 2, 3], task-oriented dialog systems assist users to complete everyday tasks, which usually involves ag- gregating information from external resources and planning over multiple dialog turns.

20b8b21c-554f-44c8-87b7-e67ca92296e7.label: text_chunk
20b8b21c-554f-44c8-87b7-e67ca92296e7.text: Conventional task-oriented dia- log systems are designed with complex pipelines [4, 5, 6, 7], and there are usually separately developed modules for natu- ral language understanding (NLU) [8, 9, 10, 11], dialog state tracking (DST) [12, 13], and dialog management (DM) [14, 15, 16].

fed589ca-e88f-4f5c-948c-34b088c83dc1.label: text_chunk
fed589ca-e88f-4f5c-948c-34b088c83dc1.text:
Such pipeline approach inherently make it hard to scale a dialog system to new domains, as each of these mod- ules has to be redesigned separately with domain expertise. Moreover, credit assignment in such pipeline systems can be
challenging, as errors made in upper stream modules may propagate and be amplified in downstream components.Recent efforts have been made in designing end-to-end frameworks for task-oriented dialogs. Wen et al. [17] and Liu et al.

df040d77-bea8-40ea-9759-56d6257b5d87.label: text_chunk
df040d77-bea8-40ea-9759-56d6257b5d87.text: [18] proposed supervised learning (SL) based end-to- end trainable neural network models. Zhao and Eskenazi [19] and Li et al. [20] introduced end-to-end trainable systems using deep reinforcement learning (RL) for dialog policy op- timization. Comparing to SL based models, systems trained with RL by exploring the space of possible strategies showed improved model robustness against diverse dialog situations.

3e1108fa-02fe-441c-9dca-ad3a9f8981fe.label: text_chunk
3e1108fa-02fe-441c-9dca-ad3a9f8981fe.text: In RL based dialog policy learning, ideally the agent should be deployed to interact with users and get rewards from real user feedback. However, many dialog samples may be required for RL policy shaping, making it impractical to learn from real users directly from the beginning. Therefore, a user simulator [21, 22, 23] is usually used to train the dialog agent to a sufficient starting level before deploying it to the real environment.

f42f2667-1ebd-468a-89c8-3d4495392d1e.label: text_chunk
f42f2667-1ebd-468a-89c8-3d4495392d1e.text: Quality of such user simulator has a direct impact on the effectiveness of dialog policy learning. Design- ing a reliable user simulator, however, is not trivial, often as difficult as building a good dialog agent. User simulators used in most of the recent RL based dialog models [19, 23, 20] are designed with expert knowledge and complex rules.

14aa9ca8-3310-4ca7-b0db-482fe1ce67ca.label: text_chunk
14aa9ca8-3310-4ca7-b0db-482fe1ce67ca.text: To address the challenge of lacking a reliable user simu- lator for dialog agent policy learning, we propose a method in jointly optimizing the dialog agent policy and the user sim- ulator policy with deep RL. We first bootstrap a basic neural dialog agent and a basic neural user simulator by learning di- rectly from dialog corpora with supervised training.

5de1b2b8-206d-4a42-a2d9-bcfced207af7.label: text_chunk
5de1b2b8-206d-4a42-a2d9-bcfced207af7.text:
We then improve them further by simulating task-oriented dialogs be- tween the two agents and iteratively optimizing their dialog policies with deep RL.

 The intuition is that we model task- oriented dialog as a goal fulfilling task, in which we let the dialog agent and the user simulator to positively collaborate to achieve the goal. The user simulator is given a goal to complete, and it is expected to demonstrate coherent but di- verse user behavior.

fc0ca8ed-3e35-4fa1-a7a0-66ea89eabf33.label: text_chunk
fc0ca8ed-3e35-4fa1-a7a0-66ea89eabf33.text:
The dialog agent attempts to estimate the user’s goal and fulfill his request by conducting meaningful conversations. Both the two agents aim to learn to collaborate


with each other to complete the task but without exploiting the game.Our contribution in this work is two-fold. Firstly, we pro- pose an iterative dialog policy learning method that jointly optimizes the dialog agent and the user simulator in end-to- end trainable neural dialog systems.

96e465bb-08ad-402f-b589-44dcda675554.label: text_chunk
96e465bb-08ad-402f-b589-44dcda675554.text:
Secondly, we design a novel neural network based user simulator for task-oriented dialogs that can be trained in a data-driven manner without requiring the design of complex rules.The remainder of the paper is organized as follows.

 In section 2, we discuss related work on end-to-end trainable task-oriented dialog systems and RL policy learning methods. In section 3, we describe the proposed framework and learn- ing methods in detail.

6a7a2357-477a-4ca4-969e-9eb738a6d0bf.label: text_chunk
6a7a2357-477a-4ca4-969e-9eb738a6d0bf.text:
In Section 4, we discuss the experiment setup and analyze the results. Section 5 gives the conclusions.

2. RELATED WORK

Popular approaches for developing task-oriented dialog sys- tems include treating the problem as a partially observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) [7]. RL methods [24] can be applied to optimize the dialog policy online with the feedback collected via interacting with users.

ed621520-a89b-48e8-b841-0c5efe33ff67.label: text_chunk
ed621520-a89b-48e8-b841-0c5efe33ff67.text:
In order to make the RL policy learning tractable, dialog state and sys- tem actions have to be carefully designed.

Recently, people have proposed neural network based methods for task-oriented dialogs, motivated by their supe- rior performance in modeling chit-chat type of conversations [25, 2, 3, 26]. Bordes and Weston [27] proposed modeling task-oriented dialogs with a reasoning approach using end-to- end memory networks.

ded7d887-3fd9-460a-b57b-72b53e70eb52.label: text_chunk
ded7d887-3fd9-460a-b57b-72b53e70eb52.text:
Their model skips the belief tracking stage and selects the final system response directly from a list of response candidates.

 Comparing to this approach, our model explicitly tracks dialog belief state over the sequence of turns, as robust dialog state tracking has been shown [28] to boost the success rate in task completion. Wen et al. [17] proposed an end-to-end trainable neural network model with modularity connected system components.

c173fed6-a36f-430c-87f4-3844d51daf76.label: text_chunk
c173fed6-a36f-430c-87f4-3844d51daf76.text:
This system is trained in supervised manner, and thus may not be robust enough to handle diverse dialog situations due to the limited varieties in dialog corpus.

 Our system is trained by a com- bination of SL and deep RL methods, as it is shown that RL training may effectively improved the system robustness and dialog success rate [29, 20, 30].

2f6b9cb1-e8c5-4bb0-8284-22581d012b8e.label: text_chunk
2f6b9cb1-e8c5-4bb0-8284-22581d012b8e.text:
Moreover, other than having separated dialog components as in [17], we use a unified network for belief tracking, knowledge base (KB) operation, and dialog management, to fully explore the knowledge that can be shared among different tasks.

In many of the recent work on using RL for dialog policy learning [19, 31, 20], hand-designed user simulators are used to interact with the dialog agent. Designing a good perform- ing user simulator is not easy.

c96f9325-3562-443f-895a-9ff4110dd8cd.label: text_chunk
c96f9325-3562-443f-895a-9ff4110dd8cd.text:
A too basic user simulator as in [19] may only be able to produce short and simple utterances with limited variety, making the final system lack of robust-
ness against noise in real world user inputs.

 Advanced user simulators [32, 23] may demonstrate coherent user behav- ior, but they typically require designing complex rules with domain expertise.

8706e249-f679-4a1e-a142-ebf85c6711f4.label: text_chunk
8706e249-f679-4a1e-a142-ebf85c6711f4.text:
We address this challenge using a hybrid learning method, where we firstly bootstrapping a basic func- tioning user simulator with SL on human annotated corpora, and continuously improving it together with the dialog agent during dialog simulations with deep RL.

Jointly optimizing policies for dialog agent and user simu- lator with RL has also been studied in literature. Chandramo- han et al.

874d42c6-936b-4107-8b03-eee254c0018f.label: text_chunk
874d42c6-936b-4107-8b03-eee254c0018f.text:
[33] proposed a co-adaptation framework for di- alog systems by jointly optimizing the policies for multiple agents. Georgila et al. [34] discussed applying multi-agent RL for policy learning in a resource allocation negotiation scenario. Barlier et al.

 [35] modeled non-cooperative task dialog as a stochastic game and learned jointly the strategies of both agents.

f04108d6-ae99-4aec-accd-3b879d110c8d.label: text_chunk
f04108d6-ae99-4aec-accd-3b879d110c8d.text:
Comparing to these previous work, our pro- posed framework focuses on task-oriented dialogs where the user and the agent positively collaborate to achieve the user’s goal. More importantly, we work towards building end-to- end models for task-oriented dialogs that can handle noises and ambiguities in natural language understanding and belief tracking, which is not taken into account in previous work.

3. PROPOSED FRAMEWORK

In this section, we first provide a high level description of our proposed framework.

d951e3c3-b718-420a-ba56-0e1463f9226c.label: text_chunk
d951e3c3-b718-420a-ba56-0e1463f9226c.text: We then discuss each module compo- nent and the training methods in detail.In the supervised pre-training stage, we train the dialog agent and the user simulator separately using task-oriented dialog corpora. In the RL training stage, we simulate dialogs between the two agents. The user simulator starts the con- versation based on a sampled user goal.

0130394f-dc94-49c9-91b4-11e444de6748.label: text_chunk
0130394f-dc94-49c9-91b4-11e444de6748.text:
The dialog agent at- tempts to estimate the user’s goal and complete the task with the user simulator by conducting multi-turn conversation. At the end of each simulated dialog, a reward is generated based on the level of task completion. This reward is used to further optimize the dialog policies of the two agents with RL.

3.1. Dialog Agent

Figure 1 illustrates the design of the dialog agent.

f2d98c28-96f2-40b6-b8f2-7c32c9f039f1.label: text_chunk
f2d98c28-96f2-40b6-b8f2-7c32c9f039f1.text: The dia- log agent is capable of tracking dialog state, issuing API calls to knowledge bases (KB), and producing corresponding sys- tem actions and responses by incorporating the query results, which are key skill sets [27] in conducting task-oriented di- alogs. State of the dialog agent is maintained in the LSTM [36] state and being updated after the processing of each turn.

c0eec19b-df70-4ede-a27d-a055426c59a0.label: text_chunk
c0eec19b-df70-4ede-a27d-a055426c59a0.text:
At the k th turn of a dialog, the dialog agent takes in (1) the previous agent output encoding o A k − 1 , (2) the user input en- coding o U k − 1 , (3) the retrieved KB result encoding o KB k , and updates its internal state conditioning on the previous agent state s A k − 1 . With the updated agent state s A k , the dialog agent
Fig. 1 . Dialog agent network architecture.

82969e29-543d-461f-b008-8ae3ac540a14.label: text_chunk
82969e29-543d-461f-b008-8ae3ac540a14.text: emits (1) a system action a A k , (2) an estimation of the belief state, and (3) a pointer to an entity in the retrieved query re- sults. These outputs are then passed to an NLG module to generate the final agent response.Utterance Encoding For natural language format in- puts at turn k , we use bi-directional LSTM to encode the ut- terance to a continuous vector o k .

cb0772db-a5c9-4747-a963-6a5c5f506e03.label: text_chunk
cb0772db-a5c9-4747-a963-6a5c5f506e03.text: With U k representing the utterance at the k th turn with T k words, the utterance vector o k is produced by concatenating the last forward and back- ward LSTM states: o k = [ − − → h U k T k , ← − − h U k 1 ] .Action Modeling We use dialog acts as system actions, which can be seen as shallow representations of the utterance semantics.

90e581d2-f6c4-4e43-92fe-3ce59b40f5de.label: text_chunk
90e581d2-f6c4-4e43-92fe-3ce59b40f5de.text:
We treat system action modeling as a multi-class classification problem, where the agent select an appropriate action from a predefined list of system actions based on cur- rent dialog state s A k :
s A k = LSTM A ( s A k − 1 , [ o A k − 1 , o U k − 1 , o KB k ]) (1)

881726de-aaa7-480b-9f5f-2055f7256662.label: text_chunk
881726de-aaa7-480b-9f5f-2055f7256662.text:
P ( a A k | o A <k , o U <k , o KB ≤ k ) = ActDist A ( s A k ) (2)
where ActDist A in the agent’s network is a multilayer per- ceptron (MLP) with a single hidden layer and a softmax ac- tivation function over all possible system actions.

c86552c1-5446-4562-a163-94461860fe1b.label: text_chunk
c86552c1-5446-4562-a163-94461860fe1b.text: Belief Tracking Belief tracker, or dialog state tracker [37, 38], continuously tracks the user’s goal by accumulat- ing evidence in the conversation. We represent the user’s goal using a list of slot values. The belief tracker maintains and up- dates a probability distribution P ( l A m,k ) over candidate values for each slot type m ∈ M at each turn k :

c37ac0d3-01d5-469d-b7d9-e47c78bda9fb.label: text_chunk
c37ac0d3-01d5-469d-b7d9-e47c78bda9fb.text:
P ( l A m,k | o A <k , o U <k , o KB ≤ k ) = SlotDist A , m ( s A k ) (3)
where SlotDist m is an MLP with a single hidden layer and a softmax activation function for slot type m ∈ M .KB Operation The proposed dialog agent is able to access external information by interfacing with a KB or a database by issuing API calls.

dbf50dbd-8eca-42d4-a55c-8e30395ee201.label: text_chunk
dbf50dbd-8eca-42d4-a55c-8e30395ee201.text: Making API call is one of the dialog acts that can be emitted by the agent, conditioning on the state of the conversation. An API call command template is firstly generated with slot type tokens. The final API call command is produced by replacing slot type tokens with the corresponding slot values from the belief tracker outputs.At the k th turn of a dialog, the KB input encoding o KB k is a binary value informing the availability of the entities that match the KB query.

f2fb8399-2b64-4473-a54e-c7cf9beb51f6.label: text_chunk
f2fb8399-2b64-4473-a54e-c7cf9beb51f6.text:
Corresponding output is the probability distribution of the entity pointer. We treat the KB results as a list of structured entities and let the model to maintain an entity pointer. The agent learns to adjust the entity pointer when user requests for alternative options.Response Generation We use a template-based NLG module to convert the agent outputs (system action, slot val- ues, and KB entity values) to natural language format.

3.2.

b928583d-faf5-4177-aaec-0052f65acc00.label: text_chunk
b928583d-faf5-4177-aaec-0052f65acc00.text:
User Simulator

Figure 2 shows the design of the user simulator. User simula- tor is given a randomly sampled goal at the beginning of the conversation. Similar to the design of the dialog agent, state of the user simulator is maintained in the state of an LSTM.

69ff04b8-61ab-44fd-b2cc-66d38379c3a6.label: text_chunk
69ff04b8-61ab-44fd-b2cc-66d38379c3a6.text: At the k th turn of a dialog, the user simulator takes in (1) the goal encoding g U k , (2) the previous user output encoding o U k − 1 , (3) the current turn agent input encoding o A k , and up- dates its internal state conditioning on the previous user state s U k − 1 . On the output side, the user simulator firstly emits a user action a U k based on the updated state s U k .

4418ca1c-b0f5-4777-b6ca-14efdaf6ad86.label: text_chunk
4418ca1c-b0f5-4777-b6ca-14efdaf6ad86.text: Conditioning on this emitted user action and the user dialog state s U k , a set of slot values are emitted. The user action and slot values are then passed to an NLG module to generate the final user utterance.User Goal We define a user’s goal g U using a list of informable and requestable slots [39]. Informable slots are the slots that users can provide a value for to describe their goal (e.g.

78d0b703-8447-4826-9967-9629c4f12a6d.label: text_chunk
78d0b703-8447-4826-9967-9629c4f12a6d.text:
slots for food type, area, etc.).

 Requestable slots are the slots that users want to request the value for, such as requesting the address of a restaurant. We treat informable slots as discrete type of inputs that can take multiple values, and treat requestable slots as inputs that take binary values (i.e. a slot is either requested or not).

f0bc333c-52e1-4bd0-b35f-81b9bca00baa.label: text_chunk
f0bc333c-52e1-4bd0-b35f-81b9bca00baa.text:
In this work, once the a goal is sampled at the beginning of the conversation, we fix the user goal and do not change it during the conversation.

Action Selection Similar to the action modeling in dia- log agent, we treat user action modeling as a multi-class clas- sification problem conditioning on the dialog context encoded in the dialog-level LSTM state s U k on the user simulator side:

33f23e2f-0f8b-4236-aaa1-7d774a1cad17.text:
s U k = LSTM U ( s U k − 1 , [ g U k , o U k − 1 , o A k ]) (4) P ( a U k | g U ≤ k , o U <k , o A ≤ k ) = ActDist U ( s U k ) (5)
Fig. 2 . User simulator network architecture.

af4f6f5e-4741-413c-b879-03185315ac6c.label: text_chunk
af4f6f5e-4741-413c-b879-03185315ac6c.text:
Once user action is generated at turn k , it is used together with the current user dialog state s U k to generate value for each informable slot:
P ( l U m,k | g U ≤ k , o U <k , o A ≤ k ) = SlotDist U , m ( s U k , a U k ) (6)

c8d9102e-c795-4094-ae55-a675683ca49e.label: text_chunk
c8d9102e-c795-4094-ae55-a675683ca49e.text:
Similar to the design of the dialog agent, ActDist U and SlotDist U , m are MLPs with a single hidden layer and use softmax activation over their corresponding outputs.Utterance Generation We use a template-based NLG module to convert the user simulator’s outputs (action and slot values) to the natural language surface form.

3.3. Deep RL Policy Optimization

RL policy optimization is performed on top of the supervised pre-trained networks.

b7ca967e-d604-4329-b38b-ca82c282ca6c.label: text_chunk
b7ca967e-d604-4329-b38b-ca82c282ca6c.text: The system architecture is shown in Figure 3. We defines the state, action, and reward in our RL training setting and present the training details.State For RL policy learning, states of the dialog agent and the user simulator at the k th turn are the dialog-level LSTM state s A k and s U k respectively. Both s A k and s U k cap- tures the dialog history up till current turn.

e5000794-bd84-46a6-bac3-48578c226c17.label: text_chunk
e5000794-bd84-46a6-bac3-48578c226c17.text: The user state s U k also encodes the user’s goal.Action Actions of the dialog agent and user simulator are the system action outputs a A k and a U k . An action is sam- pled by the agent based on a stochastic representation of the policy, which produces a probability distribution over actions given a dialog state. Action space is finite and discrete for both the dialog agent and the user simulator.Reward Reward is calculated based on the level of task completion.

4c35293c-ec8e-4989-b8c5-077ecff0a19e.label: text_chunk
4c35293c-ec8e-4989-b8c5-077ecff0a19e.text:
A turn level reward r k is applied based on the progress that the agent and user made in completing the pre- defined task over the past turn. At the end of each turn, a score
score k is calculated indicating to what extend the agent has fulfilled the user’s request so far. The turn level reward r k is then calculated by the difference of the scores received in two consecutive turns:

6ed44ef2-a2a8-46e5-b987-a2d5f6ade985.label: text_chunk
6ed44ef2-a2a8-46e5-b987-a2d5f6ade985.text:
score k = D ( g U k , g A k ) (7) r k = score k − score k − 1 (8)
where D is the scoring function. g U k and g A k are the true user’s goal and the agent’s estimation of the user’s goal, both are represented by slot-value pairs.

6f3e4227-9c1f-4075-a80c-93d78c3c13da.label: text_chunk
6f3e4227-9c1f-4075-a80c-93d78c3c13da.text: Alternatively, the turn level reward r k can be obtained by using the discounted reward received at the end of the dialog (positive reward for task suc- cess, and negative reward for task failure).Policy Gradient RL For policy optimization with RL, policy gradient method is preferred over Q-learning in our system as the policy network parameters can be initial- ized with the ActDist parameters learnied during supervised pre-training stage.

76dbdb8f-1766-42b9-8fa8-e76e4828d148.label: text_chunk
76dbdb8f-1766-42b9-8fa8-e76e4828d148.text: With REINFORCE [40], the objective function can be written as J k ( θ a , θ u ) = E θ a ,θ u [ R k ] = E θ a ,θ u h P K − k t =0 γ t r k + t i , with γ ∈ [0 , 1] being the discount factor.

71482ff3-7cb6-41e4-975b-582c9b31b864.text:
We optimize parameter sets θ a and θ u for the dialog agent and the user simulator to maximize J k ( θ a , θ u ) . For the dialog agent, with likelihood ratio gradient estimator, gradient of J k ( θ a , θ u ) can be derived as:


∇ θ a J k ( θ a , θ u ) = ∇ θ a E θ a ,

65f5621c-280a-4b24-be21-eb2f68874543.text: θ u [ R k ] (9) = ∇ θ a X a a k ,a u k π θ a ( a a k | s a k ) π θ u ( a u k | s u k ) R k,a a k ,a u k (10) = E θ a ,θ u [ ∇ θ a log π θ a ( a a k | s a k ) R k ] (11)

da1f052d-0700-46c7-9f7d-5f66531a9ce5.label: text_chunk
da1f052d-0700-46c7-9f7d-5f66531a9ce5.text: This last expression above gives us an unbiased gradient esti- mator. We sample agent action and user action at each dialog turn and compute the policy gradient. Similarly, gradient on the user simulator side can be derived as:

de944ab2-4ca0-4979-9c0f-5237db6248eb.text: ∇ θ u J k ( θ a , θ u ) = ∇ θ u E θ a ,θ u [ R k ] (12) = E θ a ,θ u [ ∇ θ u log π θ u ( a u k | s u k ) R k ] (13)

d4d0273a-c352-49af-8821-dde8eb28b939.label: text_chunk
d4d0273a-c352-49af-8821-dde8eb28b939.text: A potential drawback of using REINFORCE is that the pol- icy gradient might have high variance, since the agent may take many steps over the course of a dialog episode. We also explore using Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C) in our study, in which we approximate a state-value function using a feed for- ward neural network.During model training, we use softmax policy for both the dialog agent and the user simulator to encourage explo- ration.

8bcbd19e-74fa-422f-8dbb-4f8ec97fbf43.label: text_chunk
8bcbd19e-74fa-422f-8dbb-4f8ec97fbf43.text: Softmax policy samples action from the action prob- ability distribution calculated by the softmax in the system action output. During evaluation, we apply greedy policy to the dialog agent, and still apply softmax policy to the user simulator. This is to increase randomness and diversity in the user simulator behavior, which is closer to the realistic dialog system evaluation settings with human users. This also pre- vents the two agents from fully cooperating with each other and exploiting the game.Fig. 3 .

d1de3fd7-64bd-4247-8b09-ba973f0e109e.label: text_chunk
d1de3fd7-64bd-4247-8b09-ba973f0e109e.text:
System architecture for joint dialog agent and user simulator policy optimization with deep RL

4. EXPERIMENTS

4.1. Dataset

We prepare the data in our study based the corpus from the second Dialog State Tracking Challenge (DSTC2) [41]. We converte this corpus to our required format by adding API call commands and the corresponding KB query results.

76304f28-115e-49ad-8eff-3f454139f7e9.label: text_chunk
76304f28-115e-49ad-8eff-3f454139f7e9.text:
The dialogs simulation is based on real KB search results, which makes the dialog agent evaluation closer to real cases.

 Dif- ferent from DSTC2, agent and user actions in our system are generated by concatenating the act and slot names in the orig- inal dialog act output (e.g. “ conf irm ( f ood = italian ) ” maps to “ conf irm f ood ”). Slot values are captured in the belief tracking outputs.

c504982b-9ea7-435f-9b22-11c218efefff.label: text_chunk
c504982b-9ea7-435f-9b22-11c218efefff.text:
Table 1 shows the statistics of the dataset used in our experiments.

# of train/dev/test dialogs 1612 / 506 / 1117 # of turns per dialog in average 7.9 # of dialog agent actions 52 # of user simulator actions 100 # of area / food / pricerange options 5 / 91 / 3
Table 1 . Statistics of the dataset

4.2.

e463e719-5d93-4c9a-8352-0f26a8efe5cd.label: text_chunk
e463e719-5d93-4c9a-8352-0f26a8efe5cd.text:
Training Procedure

In supervised pre-training, the dialog agent and the user sim- ulator are trained separately against dialog corpus. We use the same set of neural network model configurations for both agents. Hidden layer sizes of the dialog-level LSTM for dia- log modeling and utterance-level LSTM for utterance encod- ing are both set as 150. We perform mini-batch training using Adam optimization method [42].

d91a7951-15e6-4238-854a-f172815fa6ec.label: text_chunk
d91a7951-15e6-4238-854a-f172815fa6ec.text:
Initial learning rate is set as
1e-3.

 Dropout [43] ( p = 0 . 5 ) is applied during model training to prevent to model from over-fitting.In deep RL training stage, the policy network parameters are initialized with ActDist parameters from the SL training. State-value function network parameters in A2C are initial- ized randomly.

8eba36ab-e616-448b-b833-c149c7c96e93.label: text_chunk
8eba36ab-e616-448b-b833-c149c7c96e93.text:
To ameliorate the non-stationarity problem when jointly training the two agents, we update the two agents iteratively during RL training.

 We take 100 episodes as a RL training cycle, in which we fix one agent and only update the other, and switch the training agent in the next cycle until con- vergence.

a0df8027-d2c9-4ac5-826e-9c689c874516.label: text_chunk
a0df8027-d2c9-4ac5-826e-9c689c874516.text:
In dialog simulation, we end the dialog if the dialog length exceeds the maximum turn size (20 in our experiment) or the user simulator emits the end of dialog action.

4.3. Results and Analysis

We evaluate the system on task success rate, average task re- ward, and average dialog length on simulated dialogs.

68933333-6b6f-4250-955d-bb23cb5add1c.label: text_chunk
68933333-6b6f-4250-955d-bb23cb5add1c.text: A dia- log is considered successful if the agent’s belief tracking out- puts match the informable user goal slot values completely, and all user requested slots are fulfilled.

3bdf2ff9-14b7-445c-a6bb-0ce7beb15202.label: text_chunk
3bdf2ff9-14b7-445c-a6bb-0ce7beb15202.text: Note that the results on task success rate in this work should not be directly com- pared to the numbers in [17, 44], as both the dialog agent and the user simulator in our study are end-to-end models that take noisy natural language utterance as input and directly gen- erate the final dialog act output.

2dbde6d2-2d60-42dc-b66e-0f5a33f488f9.label: text_chunk
2dbde6d2-2d60-42dc-b66e-0f5a33f488f9.text: Moreover, instead of using greedy policy on user simulator, we sample user actions based on the action probability distribution from the user policy net- work to encourage diversity and variety in user behavior.Table 2 shows the evaluation results. The baseline model uses the SL trained agents. REINFORCE-agent and A2C- agent apply RL training on the dialog agent only, without up- dating the user simulator.

3bccdea3-893b-4c69-ba1d-0fdc678933ec.label: text_chunk
3bccdea3-893b-4c69-ba1d-0fdc678933ec.text:
REINFORCE-joint and A2C-joint apply RL on both the dialog agent and user simulator over the
SL pre-trained models. Figure 4, 5, and 6 show the learning curves of these five models during RL training on dialog suc- cess rate, average reward, and average success dialog length.Success Avg Avg Success Rate (%) Reward Turn Size SL Baseline 35.3 2.02 6.46 REINFORCE-agent 50.

5d950785-a3c4-4cd5-9854-13ae6ef938e2.label: text_chunk
5d950785-a3c4-4cd5-9854-13ae6ef938e2.text: Success Rate As shown in Table 2, the SL model achieves the lowest task success rate. Model trained with SL on dialog corpus has limited capabilities in capturing the change in state, and thus may not be able to generalize well to unseen dialog situations during simulation. RL efficiently improves the dialog task success rate, as it enables the dialog agent to explore strategies that are not in the training corpus.

4357cd44-cbb6-4f7e-be73-66d9ef5be641.label: text_chunk
4357cd44-cbb6-4f7e-be73-66d9ef5be641.text: The agent-update-only models using REINFORCE and A2C achieve similar results, outperforming the baseline model by 14.9% and 15.3% respectively. The jointly optimized models improved the performance further over the agent-update- only models. Model using A2C for joint policy optimization achieves the best task success rate.Average Reward RL curves on average dialog reward show similar trends as above.

e539a553-6f5c-44c5-94cb-267ac5579f40.label: text_chunk
e539a553-6f5c-44c5-94cb-267ac5579f40.text: One difference is that the joint training model using REINFORCE achieves the highest av- erage reward, outperforming that using A2C by a small mar- gin. This is likely due to the better performance of our REIN- FORCE models in earning reward in the failed dialogs. We find that our user simulator trained with A2C tends to have sharper action distribution from the softmax policy, making it easier to get stuck when it falls into an unfavorable state.

1e33324f-acce-41f2-bcaf-26c3066986d6.label: text_chunk
1e33324f-acce-41f2-bcaf-26c3066986d6.text:
We are interested in exploring fine-grained control strategies in joint RL policy optimization framework in our future work.Fig. 6 . Learning curve for average turn size
Average Success Turn Size The average turn size of success dialogs tends to decrease along the episode of RL pol- icy learning. This is in line with our expectation as both the dialog agent and the user simulator improve their policies for more efficient and coherent strategies with the RL training.

59695555-bc33-4858-ba65-a88c0c1f1280.label: text_chunk
59695555-bc33-4858-ba65-a88c0c1f1280.text:
CONCLUSIONS

In this work, we propose a reinforcement learning framework for dialog policy optimization in end-to-end task-oriented di- alog systems. The proposed method addresses the challenge of lacking a reliable user simulator for policy learning in task- oriented dialog systems. We present an iterative policy learn- ing method that jointly optimizes the dialog agent and the user simulator with deep RL by simulating dialogs between the two agents.

0f6624e7-41f2-43b5-98fd-7c3130967afd.label: text_chunk
0f6624e7-41f2-43b5-98fd-7c3130967afd.text:
Both the dialog agent and the user simulator are designed with neural network models that can be trained end- to-end. Experiment results show that our proposed method leads to promising improvements on task success rate and task reward over the supervised training and single-agent RL training baseline models.

6.

properties.entity_description: A type of machine learning that focuses on training agents to make decisions by optimizing policies through rewards and penalties during simulations.
A set of rules or strategies that govern how a dialog system interacts with users, determining the flow and content of the conversation.
Systems designed to assist users in completing specific tasks through interactive dialog, often requiring a structured approach to conversation.
A user simulator is a system that mimics user interactions with a dialog agent, typically employing neural network models to generate realistic user behavior.

properties.relationship_description: The parameter sets are optimized using the likelihood ratio gradient estimator, which provides the necessary gradient information to improve the dialog agent's performance.
The dialog agent interacts with the user simulator to practice and improve its conversational strategies, learning from simulated user responses.
The use of neural network models has been shown to lead to improvements in task success rate, suggesting a beneficial relationship between the two.
The joint RL policy optimization framework utilizes reinforcement learning principles to enhance the training and performance of both the dialog agent and the user simulator.
Both the dialog agent and the user simulator are designed using neural network models, indicating a direct relationship in their development.

properties.entity_description: Task success rate is a performance metric that measures the percentage of tasks completed successfully by the dialog agent or user simulator.
Total task reward is a cumulative measure of the rewards received by a dialog system for successfully completing tasks, often used in reinforcement learning to evaluate performance.

properties.relationship_description: Neural network models are utilized in the dialog agent to enhance its ability to process and generate conversational responses, demonstrating a direct application of this technology in dialog systems.
The dialog agent and user simulator are both designed using neural network models, indicating that they are complementary components in the development of dialog systems.
Task-oriented dialog systems are different from chatbots as they focus on assisting users with tasks rather than engaging in casual conversation.

properties.entity_description: A conversational agent that engages users in dialogue, typically for entertainment or customer service purposes, but does not focus on task completion like task-oriented dialog systems.

properties.relationship_description: Task-oriented dialog systems are different from chatbots as they focus on assisting users with tasks rather than engaging in casual conversation.
Natural Language Understanding is a fundamental module that is part of the architecture of conventional task-oriented dialog systems, playing a key role in interpreting user input.

properties.entity_description: Natural Language Understanding (NLU) is a critical component of dialog systems that focuses on interpreting and processing human language input, enabling the system to understand user intent and context.

1. **Task-Oriented Dialog Systems** and **User Simulators**: Task-oriented dialog systems utilize user simulators to effectively train dialog agents by creating a controlled interaction environment.

2. **Dialog Agent** and **Task-Oriented Dialog**: The dialog agent employs the task-oriented dialog framework to structure its interactions and optimize responses based on user behavior.

3. **End-to-End Trainable Task-Oriented Dialog Systems** and **Reinforcement Learning (RL) Policy Learning Methods**: These systems leverage RL policy learning methods to enhance their performance in managing task-oriented dialogs.

4. **Hybrid Learning Method** and **Deep Reinforcement Learning**: The hybrid learning method incorporates deep reinforcement learning to optimize the performance of both the user simulator and the dialog agent.

5. **Task-Oriented Dialog Framework**: This framework is designed to improve task-oriented dialogs, indicating its foundational principles are integral to its structure.

6. **Parameter Sets** and **Likelihood Ratio Gradient Estimator**: Parameter sets are refined using the likelihood ratio gradient estimator, which aids in enhancing the dialog agent's performance through improved gradient information.

7. **Joint RL Policy Optimization Framework** and **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**: This framework utilizes reinforcement learning principles to bolster the training and performance of both the dialog agent and the user simulator.

Overall, the relationships emphasize the collaborative nature of various methodologies and frameworks in enhancing the efficiency and effectiveness of task-oriented dialog systems.

The relationships involving the **User Simulator** highlight its critical role in enhancing dialog systems through various functions and interactions:

1. **Preparation for Real User Feedback**: The User Simulator is designed to prepare agents to effectively receive and utilize real user feedback after their initial training, ensuring that the system can adapt and improve based on actual user interactions.

2. **Impact on Dialog Policy Learning**: The quality of the User Simulator significantly affects dialog policy learning. A reliable simulator provides better training scenarios, which enhances the effectiveness of the learning process for dialog policies.

3. **Interaction with Dialog Agent**: The User Simulator interacts with the Dialog Agent, facilitating the testing and evaluation of the agent's performance in simulated user scenarios, which is essential for refining the agent's capabilities.

4. **Classification as Advanced User Simulators**: User Simulators can be categorized into basic and advanced types, with advanced simulators exhibiting more complex and coherent behavior, thus providing a more realistic training environment.

5. **Updating Internal State**: The User Simulator updates its internal state based on previous user interactions and current inputs, which is crucial for simulating realistic user behavior and ensuring continuity in dialog.

6. **Application of Softmax Policy**: By applying the softmax policy, the User Simulator introduces randomness and diversity in its behavior, making the simulation of human interactions more authentic.

7. **Triggering End of Dialog Action**: The User Simulator is responsible for determining when to emit the End of Dialog Action, which can occur upon reaching maximum turn size or other specified criteria, thereby managing the flow of the conversation.

8. **Training with A2C Model**: The User Simulator is trained using the A2C (Advantage Actor-Critic) model, which influences its action distribution and overall performance in dialog scenarios, enhancing its effectiveness in training environments.

9. **Facilitation of Task-Oriented Dialog Systems**: The User Simulator plays a vital role in training task-oriented dialog systems by simulating user interactions, which is essential for developing systems that can effectively handle specific tasks.

In summary, the User Simulator is a foundational component in dialog systems, influencing training, performance evaluation, and the overall effectiveness of user-agent interactions. The relationships involving the entity MERF highlight its multifaceted role in enhancing Arabic language processing and its integration with various tools and methodologies. 

1. **Enhancement of Arabic Language Processing**: MERF is specifically designed to improve the extraction of entities in the Arabic language through morphological analysis. It processes Arabic text, applying algorithms that compute morphological solutions based on local grammar and Boolean formulae.

2. **Evaluation and Performance**: The effectiveness of MERF is assessed through case studies, demonstrating its practical applications and performance metrics, which are summarized in Table 4, showcasing its runtime results compared to other implementations.

3. **Integration and Support**: MERF is integrated with Sarf for Arabic morphological analysis and extends the capabilities of CPSL by incorporating Arabic morphological characteristics and user-defined relations. It is supported by the Lebanese National Council for Scientific Research, emphasizing its credibility and backing in the research community.

4. **User Interaction and Functionality**: The software features a graphical user interface (GUI) that facilitates user interaction, allowing users to define relations, execute code actions, and associate code actions with regular subexpressions. MERF also computes relational entities based on user-defined relations, enhancing its utility for users.

5. **Detection and Simulation**: MERF is capable of detecting matches within the MBF dataset, achieving high recall and precision rates, and simulates the behavior of Non-deterministic Finite Automata (NFA) based on input defined by MBF formulae.

6. **Linguistic Applications**: The primary function of MERF is to enable the rapid development of linguistic applications, showcasing its significance in the software development landscape for language processing tasks.

7. **References and Citations**: MERF is referenced in the publication TAL, indicating its relevance in academic discussions and research, further establishing its importance in the field of computational linguistics.

Overall, MERF serves as a comprehensive tool for Arabic language processing, integrating advanced features and functionalities that support both users and researchers in linguistic applications.
